questions.question_text: Tell me about the time when you first started thinking seriously about having a baby — what was going on in your life, and what did that feel like?

probes.probe_text: What felt most important to you at that point — what were you hoping for or working towards?
Was there anything that felt uncertain or unsettled for you as you started thinking about this?
Looking back, were there any pressures — from family, relationships, work, or circumstances — that shaped the timing or the decision?

questions.question_text: Tell me about how you went about preparing — or thinking about preparing — for pregnancy. What steps, if any, did you take, and what guided those choices?

probes.probe_text: Where did you go to find out what you needed to know — friends, family, online, a doctor?
Was there anything you felt you couldn't easily access — information, a service, a conversation — during this time?
How did any advice or information you received compare to what you were expecting or hoping to hear?

questions.question_text: Tell me about any conversations you had with a GP, midwife, or other health professional before you became pregnant. What was that like?

probes.probe_text: Did you feel heard and taken seriously in those conversations? Can you tell me more about that?
Was there anything you wanted to raise but didn't — or something that wasn't addressed that you felt was important?
If you didn't see a health professional at this stage, what stopped you or why didn't it feel necessary?

questions.question_text: Tell me about what it was like to try to conceive — however long or short that period was for you. What stands out most from that time?

probes.probe_text: How did this period affect your relationship with your partner, or with those close to you?
Were there moments during this time when you felt the process was out of your hands — medically or otherwise?
Did any previous experiences — your own health, a past pregnancy, or family history — shape how you felt during this time?

questions.question_text: Tell me about the picture you had in your mind of what pregnancy and becoming a parent would be like — where did those ideas come from?

probes.probe_text: How much were those ideas shaped by your own family's experiences — your mother's, or others close to you?
Were there aspects of pregnancy or parenthood that felt exciting, and others that felt worrying or uncertain?
Did social media, apps, or online communities play any role in forming or changing those expectations?

questions.question_text: Tell me about any financial, practical, or life circumstances that were part of the picture as you were thinking about having a baby.

probes.probe_text: Were there things you felt you needed to have 'in place' before starting, and did that match what actually happened?
How did work or career considerations factor into your thinking or timing?
Were there financial or access barriers that made any part of this stage harder than you expected?

questions.question_text: Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

probes.probe_text: What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?
Who did you tell, and when? What guided those decisions?
How quickly were you able to access healthcare, and how did that first contact with the system feel?
Has being told your pregnancy is 'low-risk' felt reassuring, or did you sometimes wish they took things more seriously?

questions.question_text: Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

probes.probe_text: Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?
Were there moments when you felt your concerns were fully listened to — or moments when they weren't?
Was there anything about the care environment — the clinic, the waiting room, the way appointments were run — that stood out positively or negatively?

questions.question_text: Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

probes.probe_text: How was the information about tests explained to you — did you feel you had enough to make the decision that was right for you?
Did you do any research outside of what your care team told you — and if so, where did you look?
Were there any tests or decisions that felt particularly stressful or emotionally charged? Can you tell me more about that?

questions.question_text: Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

probes.probe_text: Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?
Was there ever a conflict between what you read or heard informally and what your healthcare provider told you? How did you navigate that?
Were there things you were looking for that you struggled to find — gaps in the information or support available?

questions.question_text: Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

probes.probe_text: Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?
How did physical examinations — being weighed, scanned, or examined — feel? Were there times that felt comfortable or uncomfortable?
Did your partner, family, or friends respond to your changing body in ways that affected how you felt?

questions.question_text: Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

probes.probe_text: How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?
Were there moments when the support you received didn't match what you actually needed?
Did your family's own experiences of pregnancy shape the advice or expectations they had for you?

questions.question_text: Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

probes.probe_text: Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?
Were any of your fears linked to previous experiences — your own or in your family?
Were there things you were worried about that you felt you couldn't raise, or that weren't taken seriously when you did?

questions.question_text: When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

probes.probe_text: What would it have meant to you to feel genuinely respected by a doctor or midwife at that stage?
Had you heard anything — from friends, family, or your own past experiences — that made you feel uncertain about whether you could trust the system?
Was there a particular kind of relationship you were hoping for, or a particular kind you were hoping to avoid?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: Preconception is when foundational expectations about the care system are formed; surfacing trust and dignity aspirations here reveals how prior experiences or second-hand narratives shape the entire care-seeking trajectory.

questions.question_text: Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

probes.probe_text: Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?
Were there moments where you felt your voice carried less weight than someone else's, or more weight than felt comfortable?
How did your partner's readiness — or hesitation — shape what you decided to do and when?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: The preconception decision is rarely made by one person in isolation; mapping partner influence and intergenerational pressure here reveals structural power dynamics that persist through the entire maternity journey.

questions.question_text: Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

probes.probe_text: Were there things about your body you felt confident about, and things that worried you or felt uncertain?
Did you feel like your body was something you understood and could rely on, or did it feel more unpredictable at that stage?
Did any past experiences — with your health, your body, or with healthcare — affect how much you trusted yourself or the process?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: Preconception is a critical window for body-self relationship formation; latent distrust in one's own body or in medical systems often originates here and shapes how women engage with care throughout pregnancy.

questions.question_text: If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

probes.probe_text: Did you see the same person more than once, or were you always meeting someone new?
If you did speak to someone about your plans to have a baby, did they follow up with you, or did the conversation feel like a one-off?
How important was it to you at that stage to feel like someone in the system actually knew you and your situation?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: Preconception contacts with the health system are often the first signal of whether continuity will be available; women who experience fragmented care at this stage frequently disengage or develop protective avoidance behaviours during pregnancy.

questions.question_text: Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

probes.probe_text: Did your mum, mother-in-law, or older women in your family share their own experiences — and did that help or complicate things?
How involved was your partner in those informal conversations, and did their perspective align with yours?
Were there people in your network who had been through pregnancy recently whose advice carried particular weight?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: Informal networks are primary knowledge brokers in the preconception phase; understanding the layering of partner influence, peer narratives, and intergenerational transmission reveals how formal care must position itself relative to existing knowledge ecosystems.

questions.question_text: Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

probes.probe_text: Did you have any concerns about your weight, fitness, or a health condition that felt like it might be a barrier?
Were there ways in which your relationship with your body made you feel more or less in control of what was ahead?
Did those feelings affect whether — or when — you felt ready to start trying, or whether you sought advice first?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: Body image concerns and perceived bodily competence in the preconception phase frequently operate as hidden barriers to care-seeking; unless explicitly surfaced, they remain invisible to providers and are misclassified as disengagement.

questions.question_text: In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

probes.probe_text: What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?
If there wasn't anyone like that, what did the absence of that kind of consistent support feel like?
Did you wish the healthcare system had offered that kind of ongoing relationship before pregnancy, or was that not something you had thought about?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: The presence or absence of a consistent support anchor in the preconception phase is a strong predictor of care-seeking behaviour and emotional resilience during pregnancy; this question makes visible an unmet continuity need that formal services rarely address pre-pregnancy.

questions.question_text: As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

probes.probe_text: Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?
Looking back, do you feel like you owned that decision — or that it was shaped more by what other people needed or expected?
Were there ways the healthcare system — or the lack of support from it — made you feel more or less in control of that choice?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: Autonomy in the preconception decision is the foundational condition for empowered care engagement; women who experience the decision as externally determined are more likely to adopt dependent, passive orientations toward maternity care provision.

questions.question_text: Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

probes.probe_text: Were there points where relying on others felt like a relief, and others where it felt like losing something?
Did your sense of how much control you wanted shift as the pregnancy progressed — did your expectations change?
Were there things you wished you had been more involved in deciding, or things you were glad someone else took off your plate?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: The autonomy-dependence axis during pregnancy is dynamic; mapping how women's expectations about self-determination evolve across the antenatal journey reveals both unmet empowerment needs and moments where dependency is actively welcomed but unacknowledged by services.

questions.question_text: When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

probes.probe_text: Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?
Did healthcare providers address your partner as part of the decision-making process, or did that feel like it was mostly between you and the clinician?
Were there times when having a partner involved made things easier — or more complicated?

questions.journey_phase_rationale: Partner involvement in antenatal decision-making is consistently under-assessed in care models; this question surfaces the lived relational texture of joint decision-making, including tensions, exclusions, and supportive dynamics that shape women's experience of care agency.
